% auto-generated by scripts/altloading.mjs
\begin{table}[htbp]
  \centering
  \caption{Alternative eager-loading sensitivity on the deep/nested fetch:
    idiomatic throughput (req/s) versus the alternative loading strategy, both
    engines, for the layers whose alternative is a byte-identical drop-in. Switching
    Sequelize and MikroORM from their idiomatic single join to select-in makes them
    slower on both engines ($0.68$--$0.75\times$), so their deep-fetch deficit is not
    an artifact of a poor default. Objection is the converse: switching its idiomatic
    batched select-in to a single join (\texttt{withGraphJoined}) is byte-identical
    only on MySQL, where it is $1.6\times$ faster, so part of Objection's MySQL
    deficit \emph{is} a default-strategy choice. \texttt{typeorm} is omitted (its
    query strategy errors on the ordered nested \texttt{findOne} in the pinned
    version, itself a portability caveat), as is Objection on PostgreSQL, where
    \texttt{withGraphJoined} did not return a byte-identical result. The idiomatic
    column is re-measured in the same paired run (five replicates) as its
    alternative, so its absolute values differ from Table~\ref{tab:deep_fetch} by
    day-to-day drift; the idiomatic-versus-alternative ratio is the comparison of
    interest.}
  \label{tab:altloading}
  \begin{tabular}{l l r r r r}
    \toprule
    & & \multicolumn{2}{c}{PostgreSQL} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{MySQL} \\
    \cmidrule(lr){3-4}\cmidrule(lr){5-6}
    Layer & Switch & idiom. & alt. & idiom. & alt. \\
    \midrule
    \texttt{sequelize} & join$\to$select-in & 897 & 668 & 788 & 590 \\
    \texttt{mikroorm} & join$\to$select-in & 421 & 296 & 423 & 289 \\
    \texttt{objection} & select-in$\to$join & -- & -- & 802 & 1277 \\
    \bottomrule

\end{table}
